stories.title: Luxury Menswear Brand ISAIA Takes 13K SF at Feil’s 257 Park Avenue South
stories.excerpt: ISAIA, a luxury menswear brand out of Naples, Italy, has signed a 13,000-square-foot lease renewal on the 10th floor of the Feil Organization’s 257 Park Avenue South, the property owner announced Tuesday. Feil said the brand has “recommitted” to its corporate showroom space at the property, also known as the Gramercy Park Building. The length of the lease is for 10 years. Feil did not disclose the asking rent for the space. The average asking rent for office space in the Gramercy area was $89.09 per square foot during the second quarter of 2026, according to Colliers data. “Continuing our relationship with ISAIA speaks to both the quality of the space and the Feil Organization’s ability to deliver unique environments that meet the needs of a diverse roster of tenants,” Feil’s Andrew Wiener, who represented the landlord in-house alongside Robert Fisher, said in a statement. “Their long-term recommitment is another testament to the strength of the property, which is currently 97 percent leased.” ISAIA was represented by Alexander Chudnoff and Henry Korzec from JLL. Chudnoff and Korzec did not immediately respond to Commercial Observer’s request for comment. “Our showroom at 257 Park Avenue South is an extension of the ISAIA brand and plays an important role in how we present our collections and connect with clients,” James Shay, president of ISAIA, said in a statement. “The unique character of the building, its prime location and the support of the Feil Organization made renewing our lease the right decision.” Situated at the corner of East 21st Street, 257 Park Avenue South is a 20-story office property with tenants including financial services firm Argentic Investment Management and fintech company AngelList. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: ISAIA luxury menswear renews 13K SF at 257 Park Avenue South, Manhattan, 10-year term
Gramercy Park office asking rent $89.09/sf Q2 2026; no downtown Manhattan flight signals
Showroom tenant recommitment reflects stable demand for signature office in trophy asset
stories.summary: Italian luxury menswear brand ISAIA renewed its 13,000-square-foot corporate showroom lease at the Feil Organization's 257 Park Avenue South (Gramercy Park Building) for 10 years. The space trades at roughly $89/sf asking rent in Gramercy. The long-term commitment from an upscale tenant signals continued demand for curated office space in Manhattan submarkets despite broader office headwinds.
stories.impact: Minimal relevance to multifamily development. Showroom and corporate office leasing in Manhattan does not directly affect residential supply, demand, or feasibility. The deal confirms Gramercy Park office market stability but offers no signal on rents, population inflow, or construction financing that would shape multifamily underwriting.

stories.title: Resorts World NYC Adds 1,400 Slot Machines as Part of $5.5B Expansion
stories.excerpt: Resorts World New York City on Tuesday opened the first floor of its commercial casino, adding 1,400 slot machines less than three months after the new facility became the city’s first full-fledged casino. The move, which brings the number of slot machines onsite to 3,900, is expected to significantly increase gross gaming revenue and commensurate taxes to New York State, according to Resorts World. “Since the moment we opened, our slot machines have been incredibly popular with the public, generating more than $230 million in gross gaming revenue in just 11 weeks,” said Robert DeSalvio, president of Genting Americas East. “With these 1,400 new games, we will be able to accommodate more patrons and generate even more in taxes for the state.” The gaming operation represents the first stage of a planned $5.5-billion buildout at the site. By 2030, RWNYC intends to operate a 350,000-square-foot gaming floor with 6,000 slot machines and 800 live table games, a 7,000-seat entertainment venue, 2,000 hotel rooms and more than 10 acres of public green space. The post Resorts World NYC Adds 1,400 Slot Machines as Part of $5.5B Expansion appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Resorts World NYC expanded to 3,900 slot machines; 5.5B plan includes 2,000 hotel rooms by 2030
$230M gross gaming revenue in 11 weeks; major tax revenue expected for NY State
7,000-seat venue + 2K hotel units + retail/dining signals sustained destination draw and hospitality demand
stories.summary: Resorts World New York City added 1,400 slot machines (bringing total to 3,900) in its first major expansion, generating $230 million in gross gaming revenue in just 11 weeks. The operator plans a $5.5 billion full buildout by 2030, including 2,000 hotel rooms, an 800-table gaming floor, 7,000-seat entertainment venue, and 10+ acres of public space. The rapid revenue ramp signals strong consumer traffic and spending in NYC.
stories.impact: Gaming and hospitality expansion concentrates high-volume foot traffic and wage income in Queens (Resorts World location), which may support neighborhood retail, dining, and services—indirect drivers of multifamily demand. However, the 2,000 on-site hotel rooms cannibalize near-term demand for short-term rental conversions and may depress transient multifamily yields in immediate submarket. More importantly, state tax proceeds could fund transit and infrastructure improvements in outer boroughs, supporting longer-term residential desirability and development feasibility. Watch for ancillary residential demand from hospitality workers.

stories.title: Calare Acquires Industrial in Boston’s Hyde Park Neighborhood
stories.excerpt: Calare Properties has acquired One Westinghouse Plaza Building K, a 42,000-square-foot industrial warehouse/flex condominium in Boston’s Hyde Park section. Terms were not disclosed. The urban property sits within a 22-acre, 10-building industrial and mixed-use site with a manufacturing history dating back to the early 1900s. 128 CRE brokered the sale. The building is nearly 50% leased to a long-term tenant. The available 24,000-square-foot space provides tenants with an opportunity to secure a long-term lease in a market where quality industrial space is scarce, according to Calare. The new ownership is executing a capital improvement plan. “Quality industrial buildings of this scale are exceptionally rare in Boston, and that scarcity is exactly what drives our strategy,” said Tim Collis, SVP at Calare. “Being part of an established, well-run campus gives us both immediate stability and long-term upside as we execute our planned capital program and long-term leasing strategy.” The post Calare Acquires Industrial in Boston’s Hyde Park Neighborhood appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Calare Properties acquires 42K SF industrial condo in Boston Hyde Park, 50% leased, $0 disclosed
Rare quality industrial space in urban Boston; 24K SF available units in scarce supply market
Seller: industrial/manufacturing campus with 10 buildings, 22 acres; strong capital plan underway
stories.summary: Calare Properties acquired One Westinghouse Plaza Building K, a 42,000-square-foot industrial flex condo in Boston's Hyde Park, 50% occupied with 24,000 sf available. The asset is part of a 22-acre, 10-building campus with industrial and mixed-use heritage. Calare is executing a capital improvement program to capture long-term upside from Boston's tight industrial supply.
stories.impact: Limited direct relevance to multifamily development. Industrial land acquisitions in urban submarkets like Hyde Park signal land scarcity and rising property values, which can indirectly constrain multifamily site availability and acquisition costs in Boston's emerging neighborhoods. However, this deal does not move labor, capital, or regulatory conditions that shape residential development. Industrial redevelopment (e.g., manufacturing-to-residential conversion) could create future multifamily opportunities if zoning permits, but no signal of that here.

stories.title: Fresno Apartments Fetch $44M in First-Ever Sale
stories.excerpt: The Mogharebi Group (TMG) represented JBT Property Management in the sale of Maroa Park Apartments, a 248-unit multifamily community located at 475-585 W. Sierra Ave. in Fresno, to a private buyer for $44 million. TMG’s team of EVP Otto Ozen, SVP Brian Nakamura and Svp Nazli Santana represented the seller. “As a first-time-on-the-market property since its construction in 1986, the asset offered a rare opportunity to acquire a well-maintained, large-scale property directly from original ownership,” said Nakamura. “With its substantial unit count in a supply‑constrained market, the property immediately enhances the buyer’s regional presence—supporting stronger operational alignment, improved management efficiency, and meaningful performance gains, all without requiring significant capital investment.” Maroa Park Apartments benefits from immediate income upside through operational efficiencies while benefiting from strong in-place market fundamentals, relative affordability, and a strategic Central Valley location that continues to support long-term population and employment growth, according to TMG The post Fresno Apartments Fetch $44M in First-Ever Sale appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Maroa Park Apartments, 248-unit Fresno asset, sold for $44M; first-time market sale since 1986 construction
Supply-constrained Fresno market; asset delivers 'rare' large-scale opportunity with strong in-place economics
Central Valley fundamentals: ongoing population and employment growth supporting long-term demand
stories.summary: JBT Property Management sold Maroa Park Apartments, a 248-unit community in Fresno, to a private buyer for $44 million in the property's first sale since its 1986 construction. The deal was marketed as a rare large-scale acquisition in a supply-constrained market. Fresno benefits from Central Valley population and employment growth, relative affordability, and operational upside without major capital requirements.
stories.impact: Fresno multifamily market signals supply scarcity and pricing strength: a 40-year-old 248-unit building achieving significant transaction velocity suggests tight inventory and solid rental fundamentals. For developers, this indicates Fresno remains an underserved growth market with demographic tailwinds (Central Valley in-migration). Acquisition costs appear reasonable ($177K/unit), making new construction feasible if land and entitlements align. Monitor Fresno for future development pipeline as Central Valley job growth (logistics, food processing, tech) continues to drive population inflow.

stories.title: Monday Properties Secures Approval for Rosslyn Mixed-Use Redevelopment
stories.excerpt: Monday Properties announced that the Arlington County Board granted approval of a major site plan amendment for 1401 Wilson Boulevard and 1400 Key Boulevard. With the approval, a transformative mixed-use development at the 2.1-acre site encompassing both 1401 Wilson Boulevard and 1400 Key Boulevard in Rosslyn can now move forward. The two contiguous parcels, currently occupied by a pair of 12-story office buildings constructed in 1964, will be redeveloped into a landmark mixed-use destination featuring two 27-story multifamily residential towers totaling 831 residential units, 28,959 square feet of ground-floor retail, including a community-anchoring grocery store and a partially above-grade parking structure. “By delivering 831 new residential units in one of the most transit-rich neighborhoods in the Washington, D.C. metro area, we’re building on the Westreich family’s legacy of developing signature projects in Rosslyn since the late 1950s,” said Tim Helmig, Co-Chief Executive Officer and Managing Partner, Monday Properties. The post Monday Properties Secures Approval for Rosslyn Mixed-Use Redevelopment appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Monday Properties approved 2.1-acre Rosslyn mixed-use redevelopment: 831 units + 29K SF retail, 2×27-story towers
Replaces 1964 office buildings; transit-rich Arlington County submarket near multiple transit nodes
Entitlement approval removes key zoning risk; retail anchor (grocery store) signals mixed-use placemaking
stories.summary: Arlington County Board approved Monday Properties' major mixed-use redevelopment at 1401 Wilson Boulevard and 1400 Key Boulevard in Rosslyn. The 2.1-acre project will deliver two 27-story residential towers with 831 units, 28,959 square feet of ground-floor retail (including a grocery anchor), and above-grade parking, replacing mid-century office structures. Rosslyn is one of the most transit-rich neighborhoods in the Washington D.C. metro.
stories.impact: Critical for D.C. metro multifamily strategy: Rosslyn entitlement approval signals Arlington County's commitment to residential density and transit-oriented redevelopment. The 831-unit pipeline will add significant supply to the Rosslyn submarket and compete for renters within the broader D.C. region. For existing and future developers, this approval (1) validates the office-to-residential conversion thesis in D.C. suburbs, (2) demonstrates zoning receptivity to mid-rise residential, and (3) reduces uncertainty around entitled supply. Conversely, 831 units may absorb regional demand and pressure rental growth in competing Arlington and Alexandria properties. Monitor market for lease-up pace and any indication of rent compression or extended lease negotiations.

stories.title: Brennan Investment Group Acquires 440K-SF Boise Market Industrial Portfolio
stories.excerpt: Brennan Investment Group has acquired a three-building, 439,600-square-foot industrial portfolio in Nampa, Idaho. The acquisition marks Brennan’s entry into the Boise industrial market through a fully leased, institutional-quality asset. Completed in 2023, the three tilt-up industrial buildings are strategically located just minutes from Interstate 84 within Nampa, the Boise area’s largest and highest-absorbing industrial submarket. “This acquisition provides an outstanding opportunity to establish Brennan’s presence in one of the fastest-growing industrial markets in the Western United States,” said Brian Roach, Managing Principal at Brennan Investment Group. “We believe the Boise market is positioned for continued long-term growth, and this portfolio provides an excellent foundation for expanding our presence in the region.” The portfolio features 32-foot clear heights, ESFR sprinkler systems, and truck courts exceeding 130 feet. “Our investment strategy centers on acquiring high-quality industrial assets in markets supported by strong economic fundamentals and long-term growth drivers,” said Kevin Brennan, Co-Founder and Chief Investment Officer of Brennan Investment Group. Hear from the West Coast’s Leading Industrial Real Estate Experts | August 20Gain firsthand insight from the executives, investors, and developers navigating today’s industrial market. From shifting capital flows and debt trends to evolving occupier demand and development strategies, take part in the conversations shaping the future of industrial real estate. Register now: www.connectindustrialwest2026.com The post Brennan Investment Group Acquires 440K-SF Boise Market Industrial Portfolio appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Brennan Investment Group acquires 439.6K SF industrial portfolio, Nampa ID; fully leased, completed 2023
Nampa (Boise suburb) is 'highest-absorbing industrial submarket' in Boise area; strategic I-84 location
Institutional-quality, new-build tilt-up asset signals strong Boise industrial fundamentals and capital flow
stories.summary: Brennan Investment Group acquired a fully leased, three-building, 439,600-square-foot industrial portfolio in Nampa, Idaho (Boise metro), completed in 2023. The buildings feature 32-foot clear heights, ESFR sprinklers, and 130+ ft truck courts, strategically located near Interstate 84. Brennan, a major U.S. industrial investor, marked its entry into the Boise market with this anchor institutional acquisition.
stories.impact: Boise industrial strength signals broader Intermountain West growth attracting capital. For multifamily developers in the Boise subregion, this capital inflow and industrial land absorption indicate (1) strong employment and logistics-driven demand supporting residential in-migration, (2) rising land values and potential scarcity, and (3) construction cost and labor availability pressures as industrial projects compete for resources. The Boise metro's rapid absorption and institutional investor entry suggest sustained population inflow and job creation, favorable tailwinds for multifamily demand. However, land scarcity and rising construction costs may compress development margins. Monitor Boise multifamily rents and absorbed units to validate underlying demand strength.

stories.title: Oceanwide Plaza Sale Set to Advance After L.A. Drops Objections
stories.excerpt: Los Angeles’ Oceanwide Plaza, a massive downtown development stuck in limbo so long it became tagged and transformed into the infamous Graffiti Towers, has been cleared for sale. The long process of selecting a new developer after the original Chinese developer went bankrupt may finally be coming to a close. KPC Group, a Southern California real estate firm headed by Dr. Kali Chaudhuri, and Lendlease Americas will contribute $517 million in cash and credit to acquire the unfinished project, and agreed to spend roughly $800 million to finish the trio of towers, located downtown near Crypto.com Arena. “I will be delighted to enter an order confirming the plan,” said Deborah Saltzman, a federal bankruptcy judge, during an hours-long hearing in a Downtown Los Angeles courtroom on Monday. After a break for negotiations, the parties returned to the courtroom for a brief afternoon session confirming the details of the arrangement. The plan approval comes despite the city attorney’s objections to the KPC/Lendlease bid. In a May filing, the city, in its role as a creditor and governmental entity overseeing regulations and entitlements for the project, noted after multiple meetings, site visits and analysis that the bidder didn’t offer a complete plan that showed they had the funding and resources to fully finish. The City Attorney’s Office dropped its objections to the modified bankruptcy plan Monday after securing stronger commitments from the proposed buyer. The final version of the court’s orders will be filed Tuesday. KPC has a six-month window to complete the sale. Otherwise a backup buyer can be selected. In recent months, a competing bid by Sean Burton‘s CityView was seen as a potential backup. KPC is also required, within 90 days, to remove the graffiti that has adorned the tower since 2024, when taggers began climbing and bombing the towers. Long considered an eyesore, a potential embarrassment during the upcoming 2028 Olympics, and a symbol of the city’s stru
bullets: L.A. Oceanwide Plaza bankruptcy sale approved: KPC Group + Lendlease to invest $517M, spend $800M to complete
Three-tower luxury development stalled since 2019; completion timeline unclear but sale removal clears path
Downtown L.A. near Crypto.com Arena; large-scale supply coming to constrained, expensive prime market
stories.summary: A federal bankruptcy judge approved the sale of Los Angeles' stalled Oceanwide Plaza to KPC Group and Lendlease Americas. The new owners will invest $517 million in cash/credit and allocate roughly $800 million to complete the three luxury residential towers. The $1 billion project, stuck since 2019, is positioned near Crypto.com Arena in downtown L.A. Completion timeline was not disclosed.
stories.impact: Critical for L.A. multifamily underwriting: Oceanwide represents trapped supply finally entering the market. The approval removes regulatory/financing uncertainty and signals downtown L.A. remains investable for major institutional players despite 2019 stall. Once construction resumes (likely 2-3 years out), the luxury towers will deliver 500+ units and add material supply to downtown L.A. For competing developers in DTLA and nearby submarkets (Arts District, Historic Core), expect near-term downward pressure on rents as absorption spreads across three towers. However, completion-dependent demand recovery suggests strong underlying market confidence in downtown revitalization. Clarify completion phasing and lease-up assumptions in competing projects; Oceanwide's move-in timing will shape rent trajectory for 2027-2030 in downtown L.A.

stories.title: AI Company Mercor Takes 26K SF at Durst’s One World Trade Center
stories.excerpt: One World Trade Center is almost 100 percent leased thanks to the latest deal with an artificial intelligence company, Commercial Observer has learned. Mercor, which employs human experts to train AI algorithms, has inked a five-year, 25,550-square-foot deal on the 77th floor of the Lower Manhattan office tower, the Durst Organization announced Tuesday. Durst owns the 104-story building along with the Port Authority of New York and New Jersey. The exact asking rent was not disclosed, but a source close to the deal told CO that the asking rent was in the high $80s to $90s per square foot. Durst was represented in-house by Eric Engelhardt, Karen Rose and Sayo Kamara, along with David Falk, Peter Shimkin, Hal Stein, Nathan Kropp and Paige Raisides from Newmark. Harry Singer from CBRE represented Mercor. The brokers did not immediately respond to requests for comment. “One World Trade Center was built for forward-looking, cutting-edge companies looking for a world-class home in New York City,” Jody Durst, president of Durst, said in a statement. “We look forward to welcoming Mercor to the building.” Mercor’s website says its headquarters is located in San Francisco, and does not list a New York City address. However, company CEO Brendan Foody previously posted on LinkedIn that the company would be expanding to New York City in January. It is unclear whether Foody was referencing the deal at One World Trade Center. “New York puts us close to some of the most important enterprises in the world, and to some of the highest caliber talent,” Koby Twist, special initiatives lead at Mercor, said in a statement. “One World Trade Center gives our team a home at the center of both.” One World Trade Center, the tallest building in the Western Hemisphere, was built by the Port Authority and Durst in a public-private partnership. Other recent tenants include fashion and culture publication W Magazine, global financial services company Ameriprise Financial, and energy- and infrastruct
bullets: Mercor AI company leases 25.6K SF at One World Trade Center, 77th floor; 5-year term, high $80s-$90s/sf
One World Trade now ~100% leased; tower 104 stories, full institutional occupancy achieved
AI/tech tenant class drives office demand in trophy Manhattan buildings; forward-looking corporate user
stories.summary: Mercor, an AI company that trains algorithms using human experts, leased 25,550 square feet on the 77th floor of One World Trade Center for five years at asking rent in the high $80s to $90s per square foot. The deal brings One World Trade (Durst Organization/Port Authority co-ownership) to near 100% occupancy. Durst highlighted the building's appeal to cutting-edge, forward-looking companies.
stories.impact: Limited to office market; minimal multifamily relevance. However, AI and tech tenant concentration in trophy Manhattan office (One WTC) signals sustained corporate demand for premium space and worker clustering in lower Manhattan. This concentration of AI/tech jobs supports service-sector employment and could indirectly sustain demand for multifamily in downtown Brooklyn and Long Island City, which serve lower Manhattan's workforce. No direct signal on multifamily rents, supply, or financing. Office strength does not automatically translate to residential tailwinds unless coupled with wage growth and population inflow data.

stories.title: Los Angeles’ Moribund Multifamily Market Shows Signs of Life
stories.excerpt: In a market where finding a development opportunity is akin to “finding a needle in a haystack,” and existing policy can “break the backs” of developers who want to build housing, it follows that a majority of the development community has “redlined” it to curtail future investment. It’s no surprise to hear that multifamily development in Los Angeles has become costlier, more tedious, and more rare. Developers face rising construction and labor costs, regulatory challenges and the city’s Measure ULA transfer tax, and persistent issues with financing. This year, only 8,500 units are expected to be added in the Los Angeles metro area, and rents will budge up just 1 percent, according to a recent Marcus & Millichap report — not exactly green flags for making big multifamily investments. But, despite the apparent doldrums hamstringing the housing sector, there have been signs of life and significant increases in certain types of multifamily asset sales. While quarterly multifamily sales by dollar amount hit a recent peak in mid-2025, per CBRE — and then fell by 50 percent in the first quarter of 2026 compared to the final quarter of 2025 — sales volume and the number of units sold has recently jumped in certain key categories. According to the Marcus & Millichap report, multifamily deal flow in the L.A. metro improved 25 percent in the year ending in March, with particular growth in Class C properties selling for $1 million to $5 million. Private investors are filling that gap, said John Chang, senior vice president and chief intelligence and analytics officer for Marcus & Millichap. Private investors closed 66 percent of the multifamily sales volume. 740 South Burnside Avenue, Los Angeles. PHOTO: Courtesy Marcus & Millichap “There’s always going to be a lot of demand to live here, and the economics of buying an existing property and trying to build somewhere are very, very different,” said Joshua Baum, founding principal of Hilgard Economics. “Inv
bullets: L.A. metro: only 8,500 multifamily units expected 2026; rent growth forecast +1%, 'not green flags'
Developer redlining due to Measure ULA transfer tax, regulatory burden, labor/construction cost inflation
Asset sales uptick in certain types signals selective capital and value-search amid constrained new supply
stories.summary: Los Angeles multifamily market remains deeply constrained: only 8,500 units projected for 2026 with just 1% rent growth. Developers cite rising construction and labor costs, Measure ULA transfer tax, financing obstacles, and regulatory complexity as barriers to new supply. Despite overall doldrums, pockets of multifamily asset sales show increased activity as capital seeks niches amid new-supply scarcity.
stories.impact: Severe supply constraint and policy headwinds make L.A. multifamily development challenging and high-cost. For active developers: (1) low unit growth (8.5K/yr) vs. regional population demand means rents will remain under structural pressure unless housing shortage intensifies; (2) Measure ULA adds 5-6% cost tax, squeezing yields; (3) regulatory and financing frictions extend development timelines and raise pre-development risk. Selective asset sales suggest capital is rotating toward value-add and repositioning rather than new construction. Developers must prioritize entitled sites, affordability (which unlocks incentives), and mixed-use/adaptive-reuse to mitigate cost and approval risk. Monitor affordable housing market (tax credits, LIHTC, city funding) as alternative to luxury new construction.

stories.title: CRE Fundraising Reflects Distress Cycle That Never Arrived: Report
stories.excerpt: The long-awaited wave of distressed commercial real estate strategies has not materialized. That was the contrarian conclusion in a new Avison Young national capital markets brief released Tuesday showing that fundraising has not matched years of predictions for increased investments in distressed properties. The Avison Young research— shared exclusively with Commercial Observer — shows fundraising has shifted away from CRE strategies since 2022 from a prolonged elevated interest rate environment coupled with better-than-expected pricing levels for borrowers. While transaction volume is up year-over-year for distressed assets, capital is competing for a limited supply of opportunities due to less forced sales than previously envisioned, according to the report. ‘I think that the higher-for-longer sentiment is a part of it, but it’s also about a high level of competition that continues to push pricing and yield more to the borrower’s favor,” Marion Jones, executive managing director of U.S. Capital Markets at Avison Young, told Commercial Observer. Fundraising allocated for distressed or opportunistic strategies dipped 38 percent in 2023 to $51.9 billion from $83.5 billion the previous year, according to Avison Young data, as the Federal Reserve began to aggressively hike interest rates to combat inflation. After rising slightly to $59.7 billion in 2024, capital for distressed or opportunistic funds slid 65 percent to $20.9 billion in 2025. Distressed strategy fundraising has experienced a comeback so far in 2026 with $26 billion raised in the first half of the year, but the numbers are still way down from their 2022 peak. “It’s a great narrative on the buy side to say we’re participating in distressed opportunities in the marketplace but the actual pricing and conviction that investors have to have in order to consummate these transactions doesn’t necessarily mean that they’re buying them at really high cap rates or really low price per squar
bullets: Avison Young: fundraising for distressed CRE strategies declined since 2022 despite rate environment predictions
Higher-for-longer rates + strong borrower pricing power reduced forced sales; limited distressed inventory
Capital competition for scarce distressed assets pushes pricing/yields toward borrowers, not to opportunity funds
stories.summary: Avison Young's capital markets research shows that despite years of predictions for a distressed CRE wave, fundraising for distressed strategies has declined since 2022. The report attributes this to better-than-expected borrower pricing resilience and fewer forced sales than anticipated. While distressed transaction volume is up year-over-year, capital competition for limited opportunities continues to favor borrowers over opportunity investors.
stories.impact: Significant financing and opportunity signal: the absence of a distressed cycle means fewer portfolio sales, bulk acquisitions, and discounted assets available to opportunistic developers. For multifamily players, this indicates (1) stabilized leverage and loan extensions keeping marginal assets off market, (2) limited fire-sale comps to anchor underwriting and valuations, (3) higher acquisition prices if entering market, and (4) slower exit paths for legacy held-for-sale projects. Conversely, the lack of forced sales suggests owner confidence and operating stability across CRE, reducing systemic financial risk. Developers should adjust assumptions away from significant concessions; pricing power and tight inventory will persist until rate declines or borrower stress materialize.

stories.title: South Florida’s top deals: Telecom exec pays $19M for new Miami mansion
stories.excerpt: 🏆 Residential: The priciest home sale to hit records in South Florida was in Miami’s Ponce-Davis neighborhood. An LLC managed by Christophe Scherman parted with the roughly 10,000-square-foot mansion at 5545 Southwest 81st Terrace for $18.9 million. An entity led by telecommunications executive Meinrad Spenger bought the house. Built this year, the home sits on a nearly 1-acre lot and has seven bedrooms and seven and a half bathrooms. It also has a separate guest house. David Siddons with Douglas Elliman represented the seller, and Wesley Ulloa and Christopher Borchetta with Luxe Properties brought the buyer. The home’s most recent […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: South Florida: Miami mansion sale $18.9M, Wynwood commercial $24.5M; telecom and tech-linked buyers active
High-end residential market trading at $1,500+/sf (Jupiter); commercial multitenant buildings attracting investors
No multifamily-specific deal; residential and commercial real estate activity shows capital flowing to South Florida
stories.summary: South Florida deal summary: a newly built 10,000-sf Miami mansion (Ponce-Davis) sold for $18.9 million, purchased by a telecommunications executive. A 13,000-sf Wynwood commercial property sold for $24.5 million to developer Moishe Mana. Jupiter waterfront home traded at ~$1,500/sf. All deals signal active capital and strong asset prices in Miami, Wynwood, and Jupiter submarkets.
stories.impact: Indirect multifamily signal: strong high-end residential pricing in Miami and Jupiter indicates wealth migration and capital inflow to South Florida, consistent with broader population trends favoring Florida. Moishe Mana's Wynwood acquisition (with Pastis/Apparatus tenants) signals commercial revitalization and mixed-use interest in Miami's arts district, potentially supporting residences nearby. However, no multifamily supply or demand metrics provided. Monitor South Florida multifamily rents and deliveries separately to assess whether luxury-centric capital flow translates to core or affordable-unit demand, as high-end sales may not predict multifamily fundamentals.

stories.title: Elysian, Capstone go 100% affordable for Warner Center apartment project
stories.excerpt: Elysian Housing and Capstone Equities are planning to build a new affordable housing development in Woodland Hills.  Los Angeles-based Elysian Housing and New York-based Capstone Equities purchased 21155 Califa Street, L.A. Business First reported. A one-story, 35,000-square-foot commercial building at the site would be demolished to make way for more than 300 units of income-restricted housing. Terms of the transaction, including price, were not disclosed.  Elysian and Capstone plan to erect 316 affordable units across seven stories on the 2.7 acre property. The proposed project is tied to the largest new construction permit of the first week of this month, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Elysian Housing + Capstone Equities purchase 21155 Califa Street, Woodland Hills; 316 affordable units, 7-story development
All-affordable pipeline + expedited approval under L.A. Executive Directive 1; total project cost not disclosed
Warner Center location: near Stan Kroenke's 52-acre mixed-use HQ/entertainment district under development
stories.summary: Elysian Housing and Capstone Equities purchased a Woodland Hills site (21155 Califa Street, 2.7 acres) to build The Green at Warner Center, a 316-unit, 100% affordable housing project across seven stories. The all-affordable structure qualifies for expedited approval under L.A. Executive Directive 1, removing some regulatory friction. Development is adjacent to Stan Kroenke's major 52-acre Warner Center mixed-use district anchored by the LA Rams headquarters.
stories.impact: Significant L.A. affordable multifamily signal: 100% affordable projects unlock expedited approvals and likely tax credit financing (LIHTC), reducing timelines and borrowing costs vs. market-rate new construction. Elysian + Capstone's pipeline (three projects announced in San Fernando Valley/Woodland Hills) signals institutional capital betting on affordability and incentive-driven development models in underperforming L.A. areas. The Kroenke Warner Center investment adjacent to The Green suggests mixed-use catalysts (retail, offices, entertainment) will anchor neighborhood demand for service-sector workers—the demographic affordable housing serves. For developers: affordable housing in growth corridors (Woodland Hills benefiting from Rams campus) can outperform luxury new construction by tapping tax credits, reducing regulatory delays, and addressing identified labor demand. Monitor tax credit availability and NOI/yields on all-affordable projects to assess spread vs. market-rate development.

stories.title: Austin suburb selects Griffin Swinerton to revamp downtown
stories.excerpt: One of the nation’s fastest growing suburbs is enlisting a new developer to bring its downtown space up to code as it welcomes in a steady influx of new residents.  Liberty Hill’s city officials selected San Francisco-based Griffin Swinerton, a joint venture of Griffin Structures Incorporated and builder Swinerton Incorporated to head up the downtown facelift. The joint venture will officially function as though they were city staff, according to the Austin Business Journal.  The responsibility extends beyond listening to what the community wants and then delivering. Griffin Swinerton will be tasked with actively soliciting community thoughts as well as […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Liberty Hill (Austin suburb) selects Griffin Swinerton for downtown revamp; target 2028 start, 2+ years out
Masterplan focus: walkability, central gathering place, historic preservation, parking, industry-specific districts
Liberty Hill one of fastest-growing Austin suburbs; population tripled since 2020 census
stories.summary: Liberty Hill, Texas, selected San Francisco-based Griffin Swinerton (joint venture of Griffin Structures and Swinerton Inc.) to lead a downtown revitalization initiative targeting a 2028 start. The project will focus on walkability, historic preservation, public amenities, parking, and industry-specific districts. Liberty Hill is among the nation's fastest-growing suburbs and has tripled in population since 2020.
stories.impact: Austin suburb growth driver: Liberty Hill's explosive population growth (3× since 2020) and municipal commitment to downtown walkability and placemaking signal strong residential in-migration and emerging demand for urban-scale multifamily in the suburb core. The 2028 downtown revamp timeline presents a window for developers to acquire land ahead of walkability improvements and mixed-use investment. Downtown-proximate multifamily in fast-growing Austin suburbs often benefits from lower land costs than central Austin while capturing commuter and local demand. Griffin Swinerton's involvement (experienced downtown developer) suggests professional infrastructure and retail recruitment, reducing execution risk for residential projects. Monitor Liberty Hill entitlements and site availability; early movers in walkable downtown corridors can capture significant appreciation as city improvements mature.

stories.title: CMK grabs key permit to launch 28-story South Loop high-rise
stories.excerpt: A planned South Loop high-rise is all but ready for shovels to break ground. The CMK Companies’ planned mixed-use development at 910 South Wells just snagged its piles permit, and work on the foundation can commence.  CMK already received site plan approval last November, according to Urbanize Chicago. Gensler is heading up the design for the 28-story building, which will feature 3,300 square feet of space for retail and 350 residential units. Plans also include ample parking space, an outdoor amenity space for residents and a riverwalk view. The building will be connected to the riverwalk using a pedestrian path. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: CMK Companies' 910 South Wells (Chicago South Loop): piles permit approved, 28-story, 350 residential units + 3.3K retail
CMK secured $83M construction loan from Wintrust Bank for 1010 South Wells (388-unit apartment building), under construction
Riverline master plan: CMK developed 8-acre (truncated from 15-acre Lendlease partnership); $2B original vision
stories.summary: CMK Companies received piles permit for 910 South Wells, a 28-story South Loop mixed-use building (Gensler design) featuring 350 residential units, 3,300 sf retail, and riverwalk access. CMK separately closed $83 million construction financing from Wintrust Bank for the adjacent 1010 South Wells (388 units), currently under construction. Both buildings are part of the scaled-down Riverline master plan on eight acres after a Lendlease partnership split in 2018.
stories.impact: Chicago South Loop multifamily supply pipeline: CMK is executing a two-tower residential strategy (388 + 350 = 738 units) with phased delivery and institutional construction financing in place. The piles permit signals imminent groundbreaking for Riverline Parcel F, indicating supply acceleration in South Loop over 2027-2029. For developers: (1) CMK's construction loan approval (despite rates) suggests South Loop remains financeable for institutional sponsors, (2) dual-tower execution indicates investor confidence in sequential lease-up, (3) riverwalk positioning and retail anchors signal quality-of-life placemaking supporting rent growth. Monitor absorption of CMK's 388-unit tower (1010 South Wells) currently leasing to gauge South Loop demand and pricing; rental pace will inform competitive positioning for 910 South Wells and other South Loop projects.

stories.title: Therme Dallas pivots, adds skybridge to $800M wellness resort buildout plans
stories.excerpt: Therme Dallas is looking for city approval to add a pedestrian skybridge and new non-residential zones to their $800 million resort development.  The Austrian wellness company is looking to break ground on the 24-acre, 500,000-square-foot resort in 2027. Plans for the massive resort on 1500 South Riverfront Boulevard include swim-up bars, thermal baths, saunas and restaurants, all within a beach-like atmosphere with a retractable glass roof. The skybridge will connect two of the planned buildings, according to the Dallas Business Journal, but which two is unclear.  Therme raised eyebrows around a year ago when it purchased the land for the […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Therme Dallas (Austrian wellness resort) adds skybridge to $800M plan; 24 acres, 500K SF, 2027 groundbreaking target
Thermal baths, saunas, restaurants, beach-like interior with retractable glass roof; resort/hospitality development
Credibility concerns: NYT exposed Therme misrepresented development experience to Ontario government; one completed spa
stories.summary: Therme Dallas, an Austrian wellness company, is seeking city approval to add a pedestrian skybridge and new non-residential zones to its $800 million Trinity River resort. The 24-acre, 500,000-square-foot development targets a 2027 groundbreaking and will feature thermal baths, saunas, restaurants, and entertainment under a retractable glass roof. However, the New York Times recently exposed Therme's misrepresentation of development experience to Ontario, raising execution credibility concerns.
stories.impact: Dallas destination resort development with credibility risk: Therme's resort is an entertainment/hospitality play, not core multifamily. However, if executed, a 500K-sf destination resort on the Trinity River could attract sustained foot traffic, hospitality employment, and consumer spending, indirectly supporting demand for nearby service-sector housing. The skybridge and design evolution signal active engagement with Dallas planning, but Therme's disclosed lack of development experience (one completed spa vs. claimed six facilities) raises material execution risk. For multifamily developers in Dallas, monitor Therme's financing and groundbreaking timeline; successful completion could anchor Trinity River as a mixed-use destination and support residential demand. Conversely, if Therme delays or scales back, the massive land parcel may become available for alternative residential development. Treat as speculative catalyst, not confirmed demand driver.

stories.title: Tannos secures $28M in construction financing for Houston area mixed-use project
stories.excerpt: Tannos Development Groups’ Friendswood mixed-use project is one step closer to coming to fruition.  The Friendswood-based developer secured $28 million in mid-construction financing for The Albritton, a mixed-use development being built at 408 South Friendswood Drive, according to a release from Miami-based Concord Summit Capital, which arranged the deal.  Concord Summit’s Daniel J. Eidson and Ben Applebaum sourced the financing. The debt, which has a 70 percent loan-to-value ratio, is supposed to carry the project “through the remainder of construction,” Eidson said in the release. S3 Capital provided the financing, Commercial Observer reported.  The Albritton will feature 111 multifamily units, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Tannos Development secures $28M construction financing for The Albritton (Houston area); 111 units + 11K retail + 9K office
S3 Capital provided debt at 70% LTV, financing through 2028 completion in Friendswood, 25 miles SE of Houston
Friendswood buildout: City Center ($750M mixed-use, 106 acres, construction started spring 2024); broader regional growth
stories.summary: Tannos Development Group secured $28 million mid-stage construction financing from S3 Capital for The Albritton, a 111-unit multifamily + 11,000-sf retail + 9,000-sf office mixed-use project in Friendswood, Texas (25 miles southeast of Houston). The 70% LTV loan carries the project through 2028 completion. Tannos is simultaneously developing Friendswood City Center ($750M, 106-acre mixed-use district) and Magnolia Town Center ($1B, 200 acres).
stories.impact: Houston area multifamily financing active; regional growth strategy: Tannos's ability to secure $28M construction financing at 70% LTV in mid-2026 signals debt markets remain accessible for institutional sponsors with operational track records and proven markets. The Albritton's modest scale (111 units) and Friendswood suburban location (25 mi from Houston) indicate financing appetite for smaller-format mixed-use in growth corridors. Friendswood's simultaneous City Center buildout ($750M) and population/employment growth (25K+ residents) create cumulative demand for service-sector housing. For regional Houston developers: (1) construction debt availability supports pipeline advancement, (2) suburban mixed-use with multifamily components remains marketable to lenders, (3) Friendswood/Magnolia growth corridors offer acquisition opportunities ahead of major anchor projects (City Center, Town Center) completion. Monitor absorption rates in Tannos's projects to validate Friendswood/Magnolia demand assumptions; strong lease-up will signal strength, weak absorption will flag overbuilding risk.
